title: Accessible Reader Surface, Untrusted Content, and Safe Links · Concurrent Code Workshop
summary: A public-interest newsroom incrementally builds keyboard-first content-safe feed reader from original RSS, Atom, HTTP, and PostgreSQL fixtures while retaining prior Go, HTTP-client, HTTP-server, SQL, Git, accessibility, security, testing, cancellation, and recovery requirements. Planned artifact: Build a complete first version of keyboard-first content-safe feed reader while every earlier competency remains active. Learners progress through prediction, explicit theory, changed-case practice, ca…
objectives: Treat titles, authors, summaries, HTML, images, categories, and URLs as untrusted publisher input through render-time safety boundaries.
Prefer safe text, use a tested allowlist sanitizer only where needed, rewrite links, block active content, and preserve readable structure.
Use one page heading, feed and entry landmarks, descriptive link text, metadata lists, dates, language, and source provenance.
Preserve previously learned constraints and attach observable changed-case evidence.

steps.title: Feed aggregation and Go Commit to the mission model · Workshop Feed Go Untrusted Content
steps.instruction:
workshop case — A workshop Go team handles case 8975 during a multilingual university archive import. Build deterministic Go evidence to prove content-safe semantic reader structure, keyboard and focus behavior, reflow, and non-color evidence; browser Go must remain deterministic and free of network, database, process, and host effects; then change one RSS, Atom, namespace, date, identity, content, or encoding case and reconcile provenance. Browser Go uses pure bounded models and original RSS, Atom, HTTP, PostgreSQL, and reader fixtures only. Real DNS, sockets, XML libraries, PostgreSQL, processes, signals, race, load, containers, accessibility review, backups, and production behavior require explicit controlled transfer gates.

What must be established before implementation?

Evidence target: Feed aggregation and Go Commit to the mission model · Workshop Feed Go Untrusted Content.
steps.why: The decision supplies observable evidence for treat titles, authors, summaries, html, images, categories, and urls as untrusted publisher input through render-time safety boundaries. For Feed aggregation and Go Commit to the mission model · Workshop Feed Go Untrusted Content, keep Feed Go Untrusted Content connected to executable Go feed, XML, HTTP, PostgreSQL, schedule, API, reader, deployment, and recovery decision evidence.

steps.title: Feed aggregation and Go Audit the initial evidence packet · Workshop Feed Go Reflow Motion Media

Build deterministic Go evidence to prove content-safe semantic reader structure, keyboard and focus behavior, reflow, and non-color evidence; browser Go must remain deterministic and free of network, database, process, and host effects; then inject one URL, DNS, redirect, compression, entity, depth, timeout, or publisher-budget failure and reject it safely. Browser Go uses pure bounded models and original RSS, Atom, HTTP, PostgreSQL, and reader fixtures only. Real DNS, sockets, XML libraries, PostgreSQL, processes, signals, race, load, containers, accessibility review, backups, and production behavior require explicit controlled transfer gates. Append a compile-ready pure-Go function headed "// Evidence: feed-go-reflow-motion-media-42uq3e" that uses case 3873 to prove content-safe semantic reader structure, keyboard and focus behavior, reflow, and non-color evidence. Return observable changed-case evidence. Browser code must not open sockets, resolve DNS, parse through native XML libraries, contact publishers, access PostgreSQL, read host files, handle real signals, launch unbounded goroutines, or claim race, load, accessibility, container, backup, restore, or production behavior; verify those boundaries later with Go 1.26.5, pgx 5.10.0, goose 3.27.2, PostgreSQL 18.4, original fixtures, controlled RoundTrippers and httptest servers, disposable databases, race and fuzz runs, subprocesses, inspected images, accessibility review, capacity, backup, restore, rollback, and release gates.

Evidence target: Feed aggregation and Go Extend the cumulative artifact · Workshop Feed Go Reflow Motion Media.
steps.why: Building a runnable or mechanically reviewable artifact turns Feed Go Reflow Motion Media into observable performance evidence. For Feed aggregation and Go Extend the cumulative artifact · Workshop Feed Go Reflow Motion Media, keep Feed Go Reflow Motion Media connected to executable Go feed, XML, HTTP, PostgreSQL, schedule, API, reader, deployment, and recovery decision evidence.

content.title: Evidence is more than syntax
content.text: The Feed Go Reflow Motion Media structural check is only one gate. keyboard-first content-safe feed reader must also survive the stated changed case and causal explanation.

At Feed aggregation and Go Extend the cumulative artifact · Workshop Feed Go Reflow Motion Media, trace this concrete input and predict values, errors, blocking, and output before reviewing it: A workshop Go team handles case 5628 during a publisher-fairness incident rehearsal. Build deterministic Go evidence to prove content-safe semantic reader structure, keyboard and focus behavior, reflow, and non-color evidence; untrusted XML and feed content must not cross admission or rendering boundaries; then change one tenant, cursor, reader-state, authorization, cache, or accessibility condition without leaking state. Browser Go uses pure bounded models and original RSS, Atom, HTTP, PostgreSQL, and reader fixtures only. Real DNS, sockets, XML libraries, PostgreSQL, processes, signals, race, load, containers, accessibility review, backups, and production behavior require explicit controlled transfer gates. Competency probe: Support narrow reflow, zoom, reduced motion, non-color state, image alternatives, content warnings, and user-controlled media.

For Feed aggregation and Go Extend the cumulative artifact · Workshop Feed Go Reflow Motion Media, challenge the documented misconception with runtime, failure, race, or toolchain evidence: Responsive CSS alone proves the reader is accessible.

steps.title: Feed aggregation and Go Test an acceptance boundary · Workshop Feed Go Reflow Motion Media
steps.instruction:
workshop case — A workshop Go team handles case 7247 during a multilingual university archive import. Build deterministic Go evidence to prove content-safe semantic reader structure, keyboard and focus behavior, reflow, and non-color evidence; publisher fetch and retention budgets must remain explicit; then interleave one schedule, lease, fencing, goroutine, cancellation, or shutdown path and prove ownership. Browser Go uses pure bounded models and original RSS, Atom, HTTP, PostgreSQL, and reader fixtures only. Real DNS, sockets, XML libraries, PostgreSQL, processes, signals, race, load, containers, accessibility review, backups, and production behavior require explicit controlled transfer gates. Competency probe: Support narrow reflow, zoom, reduced motion, non-color state, image alternatives, content warnings, and user-controlled media.

One precondition fails while output still looks plausible. What is the correct response?

options.text: Keep the plausible Feed Go Reflow Motion Media output and hide the violated keyboard-first content-safe feed reader assumption instead of explaining the failure. That claim fails the test an acceptance boundary evidence boundary for Feed Go Reflow Motion Media.

For test an acceptance boundary, import one convenient trusted RSS feed, omit reader and publisher outcomes, revision, discovery and SSRF scope, Atom and namespace cases, XML and HTTP budgets, PostgreSQL constraints and transaction, schedule, lease, goroutine cleanup, tenant, accessibility, recovery, and controlled native transfer, then accept plausible rows as feed-service proof. This does not satisfy Feed Go Reflow Motion Media for keyboard-first content-safe feed reader.
Reject the unsupported conclusion, name the violated Feed Go Reflow Motion Media condition, repair the responsible layer, and rerun the boundary trace.
